questions.stem: What is the difference between print() and logging in a production ML system?
options: print() is for users; logging is for developers — no technical difference
logging provides severity levels (DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR), timestamps, caller context, and can be directed to files or monitoring systems; print() outputs text to stdout with no metadata
logging is slower than print() due to overhead
print() should be used for all important messages; logging only for errors
questions.explanation: In production: logging.info(f'epoch={e}, loss={loss:.4f}') logs with timestamp and level; these can be filtered, redirected to log files, or shipped to Datadog/CloudWatch. print() just writes to stdout — no level, no timestamp, no filtering. Pattern: logger = logging.getLogger(__name__). Use DEBUG for detailed diagnostics (disable in prod), INFO for training progress, WARNING for anomalies, ERROR for failures.

questions.stem: When reading a large CSV dataset with pandas, what is the most memory-efficient approach?
options: Always load the entire file into memory first, then filter
Use chunksize parameter: pd.read_csv('data.csv', chunksize=10000) to read and process the file in chunks, or use Parquet format which supports columnar partial loading
Use pd.read_csv with low_memory=False to speed up loading
Convert the CSV to a database first, then query it
questions.explanation: pd.read_csv() loads everything into RAM. For a 10 GB CSV, that requires 10 GB RAM. With chunksize: for chunk in pd.read_csv('data.csv', chunksize=10_000): process(chunk) — only 10,000 rows in memory at once. Better yet: convert to Parquet (columnar) which loads only the columns you need and compresses 5-10×. For ML: use Parquet + PyArrow for dataset storage, not CSV.

questions.stem: What does with open('data.txt', 'r') as f: do, and why is the with statement important?
options: Opens the file and keeps it locked for the duration of the program
Opens the file, assigns the file object to f, and guarantees the file is closed (even on exception) when the with block exits — preventing resource leaks
Reads the entire file into a string variable named f
Opens the file in read-only mode that prevents any writes
questions.explanation: with open() creates a context manager that calls file.close() automatically when the block exits — whether normally or via exception. Without with: if an exception occurs between open() and close(), the file stays open indefinitely (file descriptor leak). On Linux, each process has a limit (~1024) on open file descriptors. In ML: always use with open() for file I/O, especially in loops that process many files.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of num_workers parameter in PyTorch DataLoader?
options: Controls how many GPUs are used for data processing
Spawns that many separate processes to load and preprocess data in parallel with GPU computation — hides I/O latency so the GPU is never waiting for data
Sets the maximum number of training epochs
Controls how many data augmentation operations run on each sample
questions.explanation: With num_workers=0 (default): data loading happens in the main process, blocking GPU computation. With num_workers=4: 4 separate processes prefetch and preprocess batches while the GPU trains the previous batch. The GPU sees no idle time — I/O latency is hidden. Rule of thumb: num_workers = 2-4 × (number of GPUs). Also set pin_memory=True to enable faster CPU→GPU transfers via page-pinned memory.

questions.stem: What is JSON and when should you use it versus pickle for saving ML artifacts?
options: JSON is for large files; pickle is for small files

JSON is human-readable, language-agnostic, and safe to load from untrusted sources; pickle is Python-specific, can serialize any Python object (tensors, functions), but loading untrusted pickles executes arbitrary code
pickle is more portable across systems; JSON only works on Unix
They are identical except for the file extension
questions.explanation: Use JSON for: configs, metrics, hyperparameters, evaluation results — anything humans might inspect. Use pickle for: Python-specific objects when you need exact serialisation (complex class instances). NEVER load pickle from untrusted sources — pickle.load() can execute arbitrary code. For model weights: use torch.save() / torch.load() (pickle-based) or safetensors format (safe, zero-copy memory mapping). For configs: JSON or YAML.

questions.stem:
What does tqdm do in the following code, and why is it used in ML training loops?

for batch in tqdm(dataloader, desc='Training'):
options: tqdm sorts the batches before iteration for better cache performance
tqdm wraps any iterable to display a progress bar with ETA, iteration speed, and a description — gives visibility into long-running training loops without changing the loop logic
tqdm parallelises the iteration across multiple threads
tqdm is required by PyTorch DataLoader to enable prefetching
questions.explanation: tqdm (from Arabic 'taqaddum', meaning progress) wraps any iterable and displays a live progress bar: 'Training:  42%|████████    | 420/1000 [02:15<03:08, 3.12it/s]'. This shows: percentage, steps completed, elapsed time, ETA, and throughput. Essential for training loops that run for hours — without it, you can't tell if the training stalled or is just slow. Also useful for data preprocessing, evaluation, and any long batch operation.
